tax: Bentonite Claymaw is placed at species rank beneath Claymaw (Lithic order, Earth Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a water-swelling bentonite body, an engulfing fold rather than a bite, and rapid drying-failure. It is separated from its sibling the Loess Siltwalker by its wet, swelling, engulfing mass versus the Siltwalker's dry, drifting, abrasive column; the two share the genus body-plan but never the dry locomotion. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: sodium-rich bentonite clay that swells on wetting and sets when dry.
ap: Bentonite Claymaw stands 1.2–2 m mound, variable as it swells. Its silhouette is a low slumping mound that rears into a folding lip to engulf. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a glossy wet sheen that dulls to cracked grey as it dries; a slow internal churn, like a mudpot, visible at the surface; and embedded grit and shell-trace it has swallowed, studding the fold like teeth. It moves with a creeping mud-flow toward any water source, never fast, never far from damp, and shows none; faintly reflective when wet. Even after dispersal it can be identified from a polished clay smear and, later, a cracked drying pan ringed with swallowed grit, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
eco: Bentonite Claymaw persists in wet clay galleries, seep-fed mud benches, and the drip-lines under Still-water cisterns, where standing damp keeps it swollen. It does not breed; it forms where earth-pressure animates a bentonite bed kept permanently wet by a seep, and recurs whenever the seep refills the bed after a drought breaks the body. It sustains its mass on osmotic swelling pressure, drawing in grit, bone, and organic debris folded in and slowly dissolved. Range is bounded by the wetted margin of its seep: outside that band the body dries, cracks, and sets into a hard inert clay pan. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a clay bench that swells and slumps without rain, leaving fresh fold-marks, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Bentonite Claymaw is roused by anything that breaks its surface — a footstep into the mud, a dropped torch and otherwise lies a still glossy mound at a seep. It claims a single wet gallery and answers intrusion with rearing a fold and collapsing it over the intruder. Toward grey-folk it is ambush-passive; survey crews report that Pall-Brokers prize the cracked pans it leaves, which preserve swallowed bone. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
traitsText: Stable characters of Bentonite Claymaw: a body of sodium-rich bentonite clay that swells on wetting and sets when dry; unharmed by piercing and crushing, which it simply folds around; and a defining weakness — fire and drought, which dry and shatter it. Its senses run through the medium itself: moisture-gradient and surface-pressure sensing; blind otherwise. It tolerates acid seeps and total dark but fails at dry heat, which sets it solid. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the slumping mound and engulfing fold, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
abilities: Abilities of Bentonite Claymaw are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it swells rapidly on wetting to engulf and smother; it sets hard when threatened by fire, becoming a barrier; it dissolves swallowed organic matter over hours; and it seeps through cracks as soft mud and reforms. Each is a direct expression of the osmotic swelling of wet clay, so it cannot, for instance, produce heat, cross dry ground, or strike at range. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
bg: In the Throat, Bentonite Claymaw matters because it makes the wet clay galleries a smothering trap, and crews now probe glossy mud with a pole before stepping. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as a damp-gallery hazard mapped to seep lines, and its presence reshapes routes through the seep-fed clay benches, now bridged with planks rather than crossed on foot. A drying clay pan that cracks in radial fans marks where a Claymaw died and is a reliable seep indicator.
g: Bentonite Claymaw (Argilla vorax) is a swelling clay elemental that engulfs prey in expanding wet bentonite and suffocates it in mud. Documented from the wet clay galleries below the Still-water seeps, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a slumping mound of grey swelling clay laced with grit — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. On contact with water it can double its volume in moments, which is both its attack and its undoing. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a drying, cracked clay pan where the body collapsed and lost its water and no carcass.
